perturbation.original_text: If any such Confidential Information shall reach a third (3rd) party, or become public, all liability will be on the Party that is responsible.
perturbation.changed_text: If any such Confidential Information shall reach a third (3rd) party, or become public, the responsible party may be subject to review.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the liability clause. Florida Statute 812.081 specifically addresses the theft, embezzlement, or unlawful copying of trade secrets, imposing specific penalties and civil remedies. By changing 'all liability will be on the Party that is responsible' to 'the responsible party may be subject to review,' the contract now contradicts the statutory requirement for defined consequences for trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute 812.081 - Theft, embezzlement, or unlawful copying of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 812.081 outlines criminal and civil penalties for the theft, embezzlement, or unlawful copying of trade secrets. The modified text weakens the original clause, contradicting the clear legal standards for liability as the party responsible for a breach of the NDA may not necessarily be held liable under this contract.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Theft of or trafficking in trade secrets; definitions; penalties; providing to foreign entities; restitution. — (1) As used in this section, the term: (a) “Endeavor” means to attempt or to try. (b) “Foreign agent” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government. (c) “Foreign instrumentality” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government. (d) “Obtain or use” has the same meaning as provided in s. 812.012 (3). (e) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (f) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. The term includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including financial information, and includes any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof, whether tangible or intangible, and regardless of whether or how it is stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'all liability' on the responsible party, whereas the changed text weakens this to 'may be subject to review'. Florida Statute 812.081, as referenced in the provided law snippet, outlines specific penalties and civil remedies for trade secret misappropriation. The modification from 'all liability' to 'may be subject to review' directly contradicts the statute's intention to impose defined consequences, therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Party shall, without the written approval of the other Party, publish, copy, or use the Confidential Information for their sole benefit.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party shall publish, copy, or use the Confidential Information for their sole benefit.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'without the written approval of the other Party' creates a potential conflict with Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which allows for agreements to protect trade secrets. By removing the approval clause, the contract is more easily challenged as overly restrictive, especially if the Confidential Information isn't clearly defined as a trade secret under the law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA), specifically Chapter 688, Florida Statutes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) allows for reasonable restrictions to protect trade secrets. By removing the 'written approval' clause, the agreement's enforceability becomes questionable, as the restriction on publishing, copying, or using the information without any opportunity for approval can be viewed as overly broad and unreasonable, potentially conflicting with the balance FUTSA seeks to achieve.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows for the use of Confidential Information with written approval, while the changed text removes this allowance, creating a potential issue under Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA), as explained by the perturbation and law explanations. FUTSA allows for reasonable restrictions, and removing the approval clause makes the restriction potentially overly broad, conflicting with the balance FUTSA aims to achieve. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of FUTSA under Chapter 688 of the 2023 Florida Statutes, which covers trade secrets, supporting the claim that the modification weakens the protection of trade secrets by removing a mechanism for controlled use.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Parties shall be to hold and maintain the Confidential Information in the strictest of confidence at all times and to their agents, employees, representatives, affiliates, and any other individual or entity that is on a “need to kno w” basis.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Parties shall be to hold and maintain the Confidential Information in confidence to their employees.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly narrows the scope of individuals and entities bound by the confidentiality agreement. Florida law recognizes the concept of 'inevitable disclosure,' where an employee with knowledge of trade secrets may inevitably disclose them to a new employer. By limiting the obligation only to employees, the agreement fails to protect against disclosure by agents, representatives, affiliates, and others with a 'need to know,' increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute 688.002(4) concerning misappropriation under the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.002(4) defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By limiting the obligation of confidentiality solely to employees and removing 'agents, representatives, affiliates, and any other individual or entity that is on a “need to kno w” basis,' this modification reduces the protection afforded by the NDA in ways that undermine the FUTSA, because the confidential information may still be disclosed by other people.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the scope of individuals and entities bound by the confidentiality agreement. Florida Statute 688.002(2)(b) defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By limiting the obligation of confidentiality solely to employees, the changed text fails to protect against disclosure by agents, representatives, affiliates, and others with a 'need to know.' This undermines the FUTSA, as the confidential information may still be disclosed by individuals not explicitly covered under the modified agreement, which creates a clear contradiction. The original text offers broader protection against misappropriation, aligning more closely with the intent of maintaining secrecy as outlined in the statute.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed under the laws in the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed under the laws as determined by the company.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the governing law clause, replacing it with a clause that allows the company to unilaterally determine the applicable law. This introduces a legal contradiction because Florida courts generally uphold choice-of-law provisions in contracts, but only if they are not contrary to Florida law or public policy. Removing the reference to Florida law and allowing the company to choose any law, even one less protective of confidential information or trade secrets, violates Florida's public policy interest in protecting businesses and consumers within its jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute 688.003(1) concerning remedies under the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.003(1) outlines the remedies available for misappropriation of trade secrets, reflecting a clear state interest in protecting trade secrets. By allowing the company to select the governing law, the agreement could potentially circumvent Florida law and its remedies, undermining the protections afforded by FUTSA and contradicting the state's policy on trade secret protection.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract text, by replacing the fixed governing law (State of Florida) with a company-determined law, directly contradicts the principles outlined in Florida Statute 688.003(1). The statute emphasizes the protection of trade secrets through injunctive relief against misappropriation. Allowing the company to unilaterally choose the governing law introduces the potential to circumvent Florida law, diminishing the safeguards provided by FUTSA and undermining the state's established policy of protecting trade secrets. The original text ensures a consistent legal framework under Florida law, while the changed text creates an avenue to bypass these protections, thus creating a contradiction.
